result:
The cherry blossoms arrived early this year, a fleeting blush against the gray March sky. I stood beneath the canopy, petals already beginning their descent, a slow-motion snowfall of pale pink. It struck me then, as it always does, that their beauty is inseparable from their brevity. We cherish them precisely because they will fall. There is no clinging, no desperate preservation—just a full, radiant blooming and a graceful surrender to the ground.

I think of how much of our lives we spend trying to hold on. To youth, to love, to moments we wish could stretch into eternity. We photograph everything, desperate to freeze the petals before they scatter. Yet the photograph never captures the scent of damp earth, the cool breeze on skin, the silent hum of bees. It is a ghost, a shadow of a presence. The real gift is being there, fully, as the blossom gives itself to the air.

Impermanence is not a flaw; it is the very texture of existence. Every hello contains its goodbye within it. This is not sad—it is what gives each encounter its sharp, sweet edge. The Japanese call it *mono no aware*, the melancholy of transience. It’s the reason we weep at weddings and smile at funerals. Life pulses most at the boundary of loss.

Walking home, I cupped a fallen petal in my palm. It was soft, impossibly delicate. Tomorrow it would brown and curl. But today it was here. So was I. That was enough.
